speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four for Edith Danko, August 6, 1995. OK, you were starting to talk about deciding to leave Israel--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and coming to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: And coming to the United States. It was not my wish. I didn't want it. How hard this was. I didn't work there, but I got the two children. I didn't work. It was very hard. And I-- I just didn't want to it go move out from Israel. And then my husband explained to me, he don't have a job. He worked building-- he worked in a nice factory. But they got-- after Frigidaires, they didn't need the factory. He didn't got work to do there in the years. We didn't got money. We didn't got to live. We got here an uncle. And we wrote to the uncle. It's supposed to take very long to take us out from there. But the Polish-- the Polish, this was very busy. I don't know how to say. Then I-- we talked with my cousin in Cleveland and the Czechoslovakians. Because she sent us paper. Then we can come and faster here, to the United States. OK. We came to United States. We came to Cleveland, Ohio. We are staying there. The cousins was very nice. And the friends, everybody, was very nice. They treat us nice. They took us and the children. They bought things and everything. And then my husband started to work there. And he got an uncle here. And the uncle wanted us to come to Chicago. Well, he didn't-- you know, he's an uncle. He just felt like guilty that he didn't took care of us. [INAUDIBLE] And he said, you can make more money, like my husband [INAUDIBLE]. Didn't work out this way. Then we came to Chicago. And he got-- he didn't-- the uncle wouldn't give him a job. He worked-- they get them some other place. First, I was not still-- off the-- the boat to come into America. I already got the Hart Schnaffner. We was already-- I got a job by Hart Schnaffner. But somebody from mine uncle's friend was working there. Then they make room for us, too. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you both went to work there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I worked for Hart Schaffner for 20 years. All the years what I didn't worked, I paid up. And when we came here to Chicago, then we was staying by an uncle. He don't have children. And we were staying-- how long we got an apartment. If--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get your first apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Dickinson and Armitage, South Side. Then we got a first apartment. And my husband-- well, we didn't know this. He just wanted close by work by Hart Schaffner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't know how it was-- get in here. Then we got the first apartment, Dickinson and-- and Armitage. I don't know how long we lived there. Then we lived there maybe two years, maybe more, I don't know. We worked there. This was hard without the language, without adjustment-- the buses to go to work. And it's far and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do with the children if you were working full time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the children was already 10 years--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --11 years. They went to school. And they take-- that's what I ask before, if I can talk about mine children-- how hard they got here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please talk about your children. That's a good idea.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is-- the children was going to school. And they came home. Everything was OK we came home. Sometimes 6:00 in the morning, we was already out from the house. 6:00 in the evening, we came in. The children was alone home. And we worked there. And we was happy. I was happy we got the job. What kind job this was, it was OK. It was very hard with the language. And first, over there, was not-- they didn't speak English too much. It was a lot of Italian people and a lot of people, Greece people. Just, you know, the officers and the bosses that they spoke the English. The rest, the workers, they just-- everybody speak thems language. I find there what I can speak with them, Jewish and everything. I mean, it was OK. And this was what's going up all the-- the time. So the children was gone in school. And it was worse in the summer, when they didn't got school.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't send them in camp-- once in a camp. They used to stay all days home alone. And they used to cry, why we came here to United States? They just feel to go back to Israel. And they was crying every second day why I brought them to Israel-- to America. They liked there. And they should stay in there. And once their uncle came. And he start to holler, and why they crying, and why this? And my-- my daughter said to her uncle-- she said, put me on a-- on a donkey. You know what is a donkey? That's a Hebrew, I think. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know in English how to call this-- like a [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] I don't know how to call this-- well, a donkey. Put me on this. And send me back to Israel, she said.

speaker: interviewer
text: A donkey, you mean an animal?

speaker: interviewee
text: An animal, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, that's English.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is English, a donkey?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, put me on a donkey, and send me back to Israel. And they used to come home from school and to sit under the table and cry. They didn't was used to the children here. Girls like my daughter, they used to already to use makeups here and everything. And somehow, they didn't-- they didn't feel good. And then they put them in the first class. Well, they was already 10, 11 years old. They put them with children from six, seven years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they speak English?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. When they came, they didn't. [INAUDIBLE] In one year, they make the school like-- like the other children their age. They was, in I don't know, in the third class-- after a year, they make the--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: They make very good, both of them. They was in their age class. And everything was OK. Once, a teacher called me. He wanted to tell me about the children. He called all the parents. I came. He talked to me. He saw I don't-- I don't understand nothing. This was for my son. He said, your son had-- he knows what he's doing. He got to make something project. And he made this-- something about coffee. And he made something very good. He said, he made better like the other. He just show me that he got in his head, that he knows what he's doing. And that's what the children-- they never asked me about nothing. They never-- nothing. I-- I didn't know nothing. I didn't know him in the schools. If I wouldn't go out to Hart Schnaffner, I was going together out with friends. I would be a little different. So here, from the boat, next day, I got already my job at Hart Schaffner. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All right, then this was going so slow and slow. And we moved away from there. We moved in Rogers Park. And the children was going in the schools-- what higher. I mean, they made both very good. My son, he was going in-- in college of scholarship, Northwestern College. He went on scholarship. My daughter-- and they did-- they did good. They never ask me for nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Once, my son was in Champaign, he came home. And I ask him. I say, Henry, did you have money? He said, yes. And I check for him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He got $0.75 in pocket. He said, he has money-- don't have to give. And that-- and the same was with my daughter. They-- they was [INAUDIBLE] little. When they came, I mean, 10, 11 years, they weren't babysitting. It was where they picked an empty bottle, and they get some money in store for them. When we came, was this. They did what they-- they could. And they just didn't-- didn't bothered us too much with nothing. We get them what we could, but that was not enough. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's when husband left Hart Schaffner. And he went another place. I-- I-- worked 20 years. I make mine 20 years there. And then the children went to schools. My son, he went to medical school, my daughter teaching. And they did everything very nice and not with our help. And that's if they say something, and I say, don't thank me for nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SOBBING] Well, I did nothing. [CRYING] And then my son couldn't stand how hard this was for me to work. Well, I didn't-- now, I got some weight. But I was so skinny, all of us. I just couldn't. And he-- and he couldn't stand. He always told me, stop. When he got a car, he used to pick me up from work, wait for me. In the morning, he used to go in earlier in school to drop me off at work, just to help me what he-- what he could do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, not once I ask myself, how could I get married? How could I get children to bring in the world-- children? [INAUDIBLE] once-- one lady, I find at Hart Schaffner and Marx. And we talked, so she was from Czechoslovakian, too. I-- I didn't know her. She just start to talk there. And she asked me how much children I have? And I told her two. And she said, how come? They-- they killed so many from you. And you have just two children? And I was just looking of her. I said, look at this. Here, I'm crying, how could I bring children in the world? And here she's telling me, how come you got just two? And I think she-- she saw us different. And I saw difference. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's-- and that's what-- thanks, God, they're all OK. And everything is going OK with them. So far, so good with the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband is a sick man, is very sick, very nervous and very sick. He went through operations. He got a slight stroke. He got three heart attacks. He got cancer of the lung. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I mean, he got a prostate operation. He got-- he got-- everything was fixed up. He didn't work too long here. He just worked a few years. And the doctor right away-- I don't know, maybe you know him-- Dr. Clark from Rush Presbyterian Hospital, he was my son's teacher. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And right away, when he saw what-- how he is inside, he right away sent a letter in the where he worked by the Cabinet-- by Hirsh Cabinet not to take him more, not to let him out to work. And since then, he is just like this. Now, he got back a little weight. But he is still skinny. He used to be different. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he still thanks God after three heart attacks, he's still-- he's still doing OK. You know, that's-- that's what we went through. And I just-- he went through a lot. He went in so much camps. What he went, it was something terrible, terrible-- what he went through, my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you've done a good job telling us about it. Is there anything else you want to talk about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what to [INAUDIBLE] or not. It's-- it's, you know, and other things not to tell, or that it's-- a lot of things in Auschwitz what it was, a lot of things in Israel, how it was very hard the life, very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Should we--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] still, still I feel good there. Still feel something in me was I'm happy. I'm happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This was-- it was hard. You know what I said, the picture, right, that remind me in Israel. Well, I bought then-- somebody made for me the dress. And I got in a dream, I wanted so much a dress like this. And I couldn't afford one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the dress in the picture that was taken in Israel?

words.text: in the picture that was taken in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: The dress what of the picture-- that's what remind me--

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --that this is from Israel. In-- in the night, I was dreaming the-- the dress. When I was walking around, I said, oh, my gosh, if I would got a dress. I used to get from mine uncle in America some clothes. He used to send me to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you can talk about the dress when you show the picture, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: If-- if you don't have anything else more to talk about, then we're going to stop the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You'll show the pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you'll get a chance to introduce your children.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, sure, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, I am-- with pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not too much room now here for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yeah. There's a whole couch for all the kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is my husband Abe almost for 50 years. That is my son Henry. And that is my [? Lysee. ?] That is my daughter Celia. And that's my son-in-law [? Phillip. ?] And that's mine eldest granddaughter, Susie. And that's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is [? David. ?] He is not here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And here is [? Danny, ?] show.

speaker: interviewee
text: Daniel is right here. He's 21. [? PHILLIP: ?] And David is 14.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here is [? Eric, ?] my grandson, Danko. And this is [? Elliot ?] Danko. And this is [? Rachel ?] Danko. And this is Elbaum family. This is my daughter, Elbaum.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, well, as the-- as the second oldest child, I'll speak first. Growing up with these two parents was really a most unique experience. I don't know that I would have wanted it. But nonetheless, I got these set of parents. And they made it very easy to grow up with some wonderful values. I don't know how they maintained their own sense of values after what they've been through. But they certainly instilled within me a sense of faith, and belief, and strength that comes only from a belief-- a true faith in God. I don't know how they retained that kind of a faith. I don't know that I would have, had I gone through what they went through. But they managed to pass it down to me, and to my sister. And fortunately, I've shared it with my wife, who has strengthened it further. And we're sharing with our children and our nieces and nephews on both sides of the family both the-- both the Danko-Elbaum side and my wife's side of the family. People are very dedicated to Judaism. And still, I don't understand how they maintained their strong belief. It's probably what saw them through the harrowing times. I feel extremely fortunate they could pass on decent human values to us. And in my association with other children of survivors, I see that as a very common thread. And it must mean something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's very hard to talk about it. But-- again, I-- I feel the same way as my brother does, that I'm very proud of my parents. And I think the beauty of the result of what had become of what they went through is all of us here. And all of us are committed to our families. We're all committed to Judaism. We see each other all the time. It's a very close family unity. [SOB] And-- [CRYING] I'm very proud of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: They said that this was your idea.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was. [LAUGHTER AND CRYING] It was, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to talk about that a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's hard for me to talk to them about it. And I wanted to know about it. And I have a very difficult time hearing it or thinking about it. It's as if I'm going through it or watching them go through it. So it was something that I felt was very important to to-- have recorded. And I was very pleased when they agreed with that. But I wanted my children to know about it. And I know that they do. They're very much affected by my parents' experience, all-- all the grandchildren are. They're all very much aware of it, but in a very positive way. And they're-- I mean, all the kids went to Solomon Schechter or are presently still going. We see each other almost every Friday night, every holiday, every birthday. And we're very appreciative of our family unit here. [? LYSEE: ?] We go to shul together. And we really feel that-- that we have much that we're blessed with. And we really want to instill in the next generation that-- that this needs to be recorded, that this needs to be talked about and not forgotten. And we're very appreciative that-- that you're here and that you're able to do this for posterity. And I know as a daughter-in-law coming into a family, I feel as close to my in-laws as I do to my own parents. They're the kindest, most wonderful people. And it really is a treasure having them here. And they're very close, as Celia said. And, [NON-ENGLISH] [NON-ENGLISH] their anniversary-- their 50th anniversary is coming up. And we'll be with them to see that, just wonderful. [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: When's that going to be?

speaker: interviewee
text: January 21, so very soon. Did you want to say-- [? PHILLIP: ?] I feel that whatever-- what all-- what has been said is very true-- that the way that they raised their children is-- under the circumstances that Henry and Celia grew up was very positive. And the loyalty that they have to their parents and to-- and to their own families-- and both wonderful, wonderful parents and wonderful spouses. And it's due to how they were raised that they could transmit these values. And that it's-- this is very important, because it's a continuation of-- of generations that what they went throw wasn't in vain in terms of all the grandchildren, the six grandchildren, which is the [NON-ENGLISH] and the grandchildren being named for their parents, and their-- and for their sisters, and for their brothers, so that these people are-- will be remembered from generations to-- to generation. And the closeness that we all feel towards pop and babi is something that is-- can't be-- can't be really described-- and that the whole family is involved with knowing and living the Holocaust experience. My son, who's not here, was interviewed for a PhD Social Work dissertation about grandchildren of Holocaust survivors. And so this is something that affects all of us and that we're very proud to-- to be a part of this continuation of generations. And that we love our pop and babi very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [? LYSEE: ?] Eema-- Yes? [? LYSEE: ?] Did you mention that each of the grandchildren are named for Papa's and-- my-- my Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in-laws'--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- that's what I--

speaker: interviewee
text: --brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's what I want to now mention that all the children-- and I want to thank you, they did this, guys. [LAUGHTER] I'm glad. And what I told about my children, what they did everything for themselves. We didn't help them too much. See, my daughter, my son-- they understood-- I feel sorry for them. They understood too much. [INAUDIBLE] They-- they didn't ask for nothing. They went through school. They did what they could. And the- the-- and the names-- I really thank you for all of you what you did.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Susie ?] comes from--

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Susie-- ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Zisl [? Debra, ?] which are two sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the younger sister. You remember what I told you?

speaker: interviewer
text: The nine-year-old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. What-- what I told you, she was smarter than me. She understood more like I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she's the oldest granddaughter, and she's named--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do-- do you want to talk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zisl [? Debra. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm named after the oldest and the youngest of my grandmother's sisters. Zisl was the oldest, and [? Debra ?] was the youngest. Each of us are named after their siblings.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our son Daniel is named-- [? Daniel ?] [? Velvel-- ?] is named after two brothers of my father. And our youngest is named after [? Phil's ?] family, David-- David [? Gavriel. ?] And then do you want to tell about-- [? LYSEE: ?] Henry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, [INAUDIBLE] [? Eric-- ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Eric-- ?] [? Eric, ?] why don't you tell us what your Hebrew name is? [? ERIC: ?] OK, my Hebrew name is [? Asher ?] [? Ber. ?] And I'm named-- sorry-- and [? Asher ?] is for Anshel. It's one of the-- it's one of their brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: One of Papa's-- [? ERIC: ?] Papa's brothers--

speaker: interviewee
text: My brothers. [? ERIC: ?] Oh, one of babi's brothers. And [? Ber ?] is for [? Berek. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Beryl. ?] [? Beryl. ?] [? ERIC: ?] I thought you said it was [? Berek. ?] [? LYSEE: ?] [? Beryl-- ?] no, [? Beryl. ?] [? ERIC: ?] OK, and [? Beryl, ?] one of papa's brothers. Mm-hm. [? ERIC: ?] And they both died in the Holocaust, so that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where does [? Elliot ?] come from? [? LYSEE: ?] [? Elliot ?] is [? Hillel ?] Leib. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: [? ELLIOT: ?] And I have no clue. [? LYSEE: ?] Well, no. [? Hillel ?] is actually for a grandfather of mine. But Leib is for a brother of eema's.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother. Leib was the oldest brother. [? LYSEE: ?] And [? Rachel ?] [? Sara. ?] Is [? Rachel ?] [? Sara. ?] It was mine sister what I told you the story about her, how she-- she died. Her name is [? Rachel ?] [? Sara. ?] That's all. I got just I think two brothers. And they don't have no names. My husband got all him's brothers the names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, unfortunately-- and I know you had an assortment of names to choose from.

speaker: interviewee
text: Too many.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [? LYSEE: ?] And we wish we didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [INAUDIBLE] that's what I really thank for my-- for my children to do this. Not all the children did this, what they did.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to disagree with my mother, if I may. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: She will repeatedly make one particular mistake, and that is claim that she did not give us anything--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --that we are self-made. But she's very wrong about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Agreed.

speaker: interviewee
text: They did not have material goods to give us. They didn't have major financial assets to share with us. Well, my sister and I went through school on loans, and scholarships. And we worked part-time. But the drive that it took to get through, on my part, medical school, with loans and three part-time jobs at once, you know-- I mean, that kind of a value was instilled in me by my father, who worked 12 hours a day six days a week.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- then worked [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother would go to work taking buses, and L's, and taking two hours to get to work in all sorts of horrid weather. You know, she had to go to work. My very first year as an intern, I made more money that year than my parents combined income in any year of their work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they didn't have things to give us. But they gave us a great deal more than we could count on paper. They gave us the desire, the ability.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? LYSEE: ?] And love. So I have to disagree with my mother. She doesn't realize--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: --how much they really did give us.

speaker: interviewee
text: She definitely underestimates their--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. You tell it. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: She underestimate what she gave us, she really does.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't give what other parents give for children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she sees what other Americans parents give with the cars, and the clothes, and the trips. But it's something we never knew about, never expected, never thought we deserved it or earned it. It's something that we did it on our own. And we never really felt an expectation for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you'll be able to give those things to the next generation--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we do. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: --who will expect it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we spoil them willingly and unwilling also.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, great.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our daughter-- should I just tell you? Our daughter now goes to medical school, which I think is worthy of mentioning. And her brother who's not here is finishing his last year at the University of Illinois. [? LYSEE: ?] And he'll be going to law school. And he'll be going to law school. And-- [? PHILLIP: ?] [? David. ?] And [? David ?] was bar mitzvahed. I kind of have to say something about my two boys who are not here. And [? David, ?] who is 14, will be starting high school. And he's at Camp Ramah for the summer. That's why he's not here.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? LYSEE: ?] Would you mention the invitation that [? David ?] wrote?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, go ahead. You go ahead. [? LYSEE: ?] No, you-- you go ahead. For his bar mitzvah, he wanted to honor my parents. And the invitation itself was-- it was his wording. It was in honor of my parents, Abe and Edith Danko. And then it was, in memory of my grandmother, Helen Elbaum. I would like you to come and share bar mitzvah. So that was special.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what he decided.

speaker: interviewer
text: Actually, we saw those on the-- [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: You saw them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, you saw them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was his idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was very nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got both-- both--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was his idea.

speaker: interviewee
text: --both invitations there of the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And he just came from Israel, [? Eric. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Want to talk about Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? ERIC: ?] OK, it was a very moving experience. It was very fun, but most of all moving to see where-- where thousands of years ago, all these prophets I'm learning about-- and everyone mentions them-- where they have been. And-- [? LYSEE: ?] You got to meet cousins of papa and babi's. [? ERIC: ?] Yeah, I got to meet cousins from everywhere, a lot of cousins. And-- It's [INAUDIBLE]. [? LYSEE: ?] And you felt proud that you were a Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? ERIC: ?] Yeah. Great. Anything else? We need to wrap it up. OK, great. Thank you so much.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just want to thank you for the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for doing this. [? LYSEE: ?] Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for doing this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thank for mine all children they came here. And they are very nice. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: They're very nice-- [INAUDIBLE] they really. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of times, I feel sorry for children from the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, eema!

speaker: interviewee
text: --from the-- from the Holocaust parents. What I want to say about the pictures-- I wanted so a dress like this. I got-- I dreamed in the night that I have the dress. And it took me maybe about three, four years how long I bought the material. And a woman, a neighbor [INAUDIBLE] she make me the dress. And she didn't charge me even for the dress. And I was so happy. I-- I just-- I don't know. And I will say, I think mine children know her-- the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She make my dress in-- in Israel. [INAUDIBLE] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know. It's finished.
